{{Short description="Antiparticle of the electron with positive electric charge}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Positron
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Antimatter]], [[Electron]], [[Elementary particle]], [[Beta decay]]
| composition = [[Elementary particle]]
| statistics = [[Fermion]]
| symbol = e⁺
| mass = 9.1093837015×10⁻³¹ kg (0.511 MeV/c²)
| charge = +1 e
| spin = 1/2
}}

The '''positron''' (or '''antielectron''') is the [[antiparticle]] of the [[electron]]. It has the same [[mass]] and [[spin (physics)|spin]] as the electron but opposite [[electric charge]] (+1 [[elementary charge|e]] instead of −1 e). The positron was the first [[antimatter]] particle to be discovered, predicted by [[Paul Dirac]] in 1928 and experimentally observed by [[Carl Anderson]] in 1932.

== Discovery ==
[[Carl Anderson]] observed a particle in a [[cloud chamber]] with the same mass as an electron but curving oppositely in a [[magnetic field]]. He named it the "positron." Dirac's theoretical prediction and Anderson's experimental confirmation earned [[Nobel Prize in Physics|Nobel Prizes]] in 1933 and 1936 respectively.

== Properties ==
* Mass: exactly equal to the electron mass (0.511 MeV/c²)
* Charge: +1 e (opposite sign of electron)
* Spin: 1/2 (fermion)
* Stable in vacuum, but annihilates rapidly in matter

== Annihilation ==
When a positron encounters an electron, they annihilate, producing two (or sometimes three) [[gamma ray]] [[photon]]s:

:<math>e^+ + e^- \rightarrow \gamma + \gamma</math>

Each photon has energy 511 keV (the rest mass energy of the electron/positron). [[Conservation of momentum]] requires two photons emitted in opposite directions.

== Production ==

=== Beta-plus decay ===
:<math>p \rightarrow n + e^+ + \nu_e</math>
Proton-rich nuclei emit positrons.

=== Pair production ===
A high-energy photon (>1.022 MeV) interacting with a nucleus can create an electron–positron pair:
:<math>\gamma \rightarrow e^+ + e^-</math>

=== Artificial production ===
* [[Particle accelerator]]s
* [[PET scan|PET]] radioisotopes (e.g., <sup>18</sup>F, <sup>11</sup>C)

== Applications ==
* '''[[PET scan]]s''' (Positron Emission Tomography) — medical imaging using positron-emitting radioisotopes
* '''Positron annihilation spectroscopy''' — materials science, defect detection
* '''[[Positronium]]''' studies — bound electron–positron system for testing QED
* '''[[Antihydrogen]]''' production — combining positrons with antiprotons

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Antimatter]]
* [[Beta decay]]
* [[Electron]]
* [[Gamma ray]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Positron| ]]
[[Category:Antimatter]]
[[Category:Leptons]]
[[Category:Elementary particles]]
